speaker: interviewer
text: Kurt Saunders. August 16, 1996. The-- during all this time that you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in prison or in-- I know that you had some contact with your parents. Did you know what was happening to them? Was anything of any serious--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing at that particular time. We corresponded. But nothing happened. My parents happened a few months later, Christmas time. They tried to escape too. That's when it really started again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So we'll get back to them later. What happened within the-- how did you use those five days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were released-- well, sitting in prison there, it's a-- it's very hard when you're sitting in a cell and your mind, your wheels are turning. What are you going to do if they deport you? Because on the first day, I didn't get the questionnaire. It was on the third day.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, your mind works. It runs away with you. At night, you want to go to sleep, it's running away. You figure out are they going to separate me from my brother? We were separated in prison. But if they deport us, are they going to deport us together, going to separate us? What's going to happen? I mean, you worry. You-- you're not going to have an empty head. It keeps going. You can't sleep at night, because you're thinking, thinking. What I'm going to do? How am I going to handle that, everything else. So how did you handle it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after five days, they gave me the piece of paper. Now, next thing to do is I went to the committee, the Jewish committee in Brussels, and said what am I going to do? If they listen to this, you'll know under League of Nations, this is the rules. This is what's going to happen. What can I do? Well, we have made arrangements, was the answer. They're going to empty a building at Merksplas. I said, what's Merksplas. That's a maximum prison, because in Belgium, nobody gets executed. If you're sentenced to death, you're-- it's changed to life in prison. But as far as your family's concerned, you're dead. The only time-- yes. This is what our governor here in New York State wanted to do too.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but I don't know how-- how this would solve your problem, so you have to tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they tell us, this is a maximum prison. That's where they're going-- that's where you're going to. But you're not going to be in the prison. They also have a prison-- prison farm. There's a building that has been emptied out. You and other refugees are going to be-- if you volunteer. You don't have to go. You don't have to do what we tell you to do. But if you go there, you'll be safe for a while. You'll have one guard with you. You're not criminals. You'll work on the farm. And that's where you'll live and they're going to feed you and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I had no choice. Choice did I have? So both of us signed and we went there. Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Merksplas at the Dutch border, on the Holland border. Merksplas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mex?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Merksplas.

speaker: interviewee
text: M-E-R-K-S-P-L-A-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the National Guard spoke Flemish, not French. And I learned to curse in Flemish, [LAUGHS] all the nice words that you learn in every language. We had a nice guard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you, other than your brother? Who were the other--

speaker: interviewee
text: I discovered other things there. I discovered a cousin of mine from Berlin, my first cousin, my father's sister's son. I had an aunt that lived in Berlin. He was there. He had escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: I found out soon enough that my uncle that I mentioned as the Communist had his brother-in-law there. When the brother-in-law heard about me, I put down a name, my uncle, he says, did you know you endangered your uncle? I said, what do you mean? He's in Belgium organizing the underground. I said, you're kidding. But how should I know? As far as I was concerned, he was in Russia. Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so here I am in the place and we are starting. I'm working on the farm, digging beets and piling them up and then covering it with earth. Also, there was a calf being born. I helped to deliver the calf. I learned how to ride on a Belgian horse bareback.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several months. And it was fun. Really, in a way it was fun. The guys formed a group, a theater group to perform. We had a dancer. They did musicians to create the bolero and they danced. And my brother was handling the spotlights and things. But also there were problems. We got the food from the prison. So once a day, guys would go in there and carry the food out. Naturally, the prisoners that did the cooking gave us the leftovers, horrible-tasting food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jewish committee came down from Brussel to see how we're doing. And I had become involved. I always become involved in things. I organized a youth group. It was 16-year-old [INAUDIBLE], from 16 to 25. I organized them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we sat in the dining room, dining room-- we called it the dining room, at the table, the wooden tables. And when the committee came in, we took the cup, the coffee cups, the coffee. It was called coffee. We banged them on the table and we protested and we insisted, we don't want any special treatment but let us do our own cooking. Give us the food that you give to the prisoners. Set up a stove here, boiler, big kettles, that's what they used. We do our own cooking. Also, winter's coming. We have no clothing. There's only one big belly stove in this big stone building. And we got some clothing from the Jewish committee. And another thing, we're not criminals. We would like to be able to get out. So after talking and talking with the Belgian police and everything else, we were permitted to go out once every two weeks, either to Antwerp or to Brussel.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in this farm?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 60. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was everybody Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Actually, all refugees. All refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your goal when you were there? Did you know where you wanted to go or what you wanted to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go to America, the United States. Now we were getting affidavits. But also, they gave us one stamp a week, an international stamp so we could write letters. And some guys didn't write letters. Some others couldn't make a-- we had to-- like in the army, we had to make up our bunk. We had to shine our shoes. So I shined shoes for guys and I made up their bunks and I got stamps in exchange. They had no money. So I had stamps to write to America and write to my parents too and so on. And finally we promised the affidavits are coming. The affidavits came, but then you have numbers to wait for. And anyway, I had become friendly with the daughter of the president of the Jewish committee. And she used to come more often to visit us. And each time she visited, she said hello to me. And I found out that they're going to start another camp in England called the Kitchener Camp outside Sandwich-- Lord Kitchener, famous lord-- and that they've taken seven refugees that have camp experience from Belgium and seven they're taking from Holland. So I managed to get my brother and myself on that list.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me a time frame. What year was that now?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still in 19-- it was beginning of '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can look at the papers, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's OK. Giving some idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was beginning of '39. And we were sent to England, start the Kitchener camp. We started it. It was the beginning of it. And naturally this was a complete different setup now. We slept in barracks, because it was an army camp. And food was different. We cooked our own food. As a matter of fact, we had a Jewish cook who had been in the British Navy. He was an excellent cook. Naturally again we had officers that got better food than we did. And I learned some English from him, like I learned the Dutch before, the Flemish. Naturally I started of with the odd words in every language. He even had a name for me. I couldn't mention that you to before. You asked me. I don't think it was good. It was a four-letter word first and then boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people who were in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Young, old? Where were they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Young. Germans and Austrian refugees. And the man in charge was Lord Reading. Lord Reading was a Jewish-- was a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the goal there, to get-- still to get to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: The goal there-- if you looked at my passport, the only refugees who were permitted to go in were refugees in transfer. You had to have papers. We had papers already to go to America with affidavits already. Otherwise the English would not let you in. You had to have-- my passport was stamped for transfer only. Transition, not transfer. So it was only temporary stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a time limit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no time limit. At least we weren't told about the time limit. The thing that happened before, that the war broke out. War broke out in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right in September.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then they offered us to join the British army. And there were two questions I asked. I asked one thing, will I be a British citizen? And they said, no. You go back to Austria. I said, I'm not interested to go back to Austria. And then I said, are we joining the British Army, because still I might have been forced to join the British Army later on, because we were classified in different A, B, and C. Some were interned immediately. Some had to limit how many miles they could travel from that camp. And others had an unrestricted area that they could travel in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, are we joining the British Army? Yes. You're going to be part of the Pioneer Corps. So I said, what's the Pioneer Corps. Well, you blow up fortifications. Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: When you fight the Germans, enhanced group. As a matter of fact, 50 years later, the British finally admitted that they had Germans, they called them, the Jews, that landed on the-- on Normandy. They had 140 of them that blew up fortifications. That was the Pioneer Corps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you free at liberty not to join the British Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I had to also-- I didn't know at that time, but they told us that some of us would be interned. I happened to be in the classification I wasn't interned. But I know of people that I met there that are interned, that I met after the war. Some of them were shipped to the Isle of Man, which was very unique, Christians, Germans were interned there, German sympathizer Nazis, and the Jews in the same camp, German Jews and Austrian Jews. And others were shipped to Canada. You must know about that too. Sent to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: As enemy aliens or as Austrians or as British or as English refugees?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We were not considered. Once the war broke out, you were not a refugee anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were German or an Austrian in their eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, to go back to before I went for England, while I was still in Merksplas, Christmas time, I got a phone-- I was asked to come to the warden's office. Got to the warden's office, he said, you have a call from Cologne, Germany. My father was on the phone. The next step was for them to come to Belgium too. And they wanted to know who they should get in touch with, how they should continue. And they followed. They got there Christmas time they got into Belgium. And we did-- we had made the arrangements that we could leave the camp, I had my parents. And that was the last time I saw them, because when I was in England, again we tried to get them to England. The only way the English would accept them was as servants. I got him a job. My father would have been the gardener. My mother would have been the housekeeper and so on. And then the war broke out. And automatically, they became now the enemy. In the eyes of the British-- at least this is the way it was used-- they were Germans. They had become Germans by the Anschluss. Austrians got all German passports. And they didn't care Jews, Jews. As a matter of fact, in England, there were pictures-- there was a film made about the concentration camps and the concentration camps weren't gas chambers yet. And I was there and the British would not let us show the movies. They said, these are our cousins. We don't want to offend them. It was before the war broke out. And then when the war broke out, it was a different story, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave for the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left in 1940, in February of 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the Pioneer-- the--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Kitchener Camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kitchener Camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I arrived in New York on Washington's birthday, February 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who brought you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandfather was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Since 1904, my mother's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever seen him before?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was an Orthodox Jew who prepared children for the bar mitzvah. He belonged to a real shul. He lived on 102nd Street between Second Avenue and First Avenue, Manhattan. He was a real Orthodox, old-fashioned religious Jew. Little man. And actually, the people that we got-- that really got the papers for us and so on wasn't he. That was his granddaughter and his daughter-in-law that were pushing and pushing. And the papers were made out-- because he was not wealthy, were made out by the president of the shul, who was a wholesale butter and egg dealer on Second Avenue. And that's how we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you-- when you got to this country? No. I want to go back and ask one more question about your stay in Camp Kitchener. To what extent were you able to lead a Jewish life of any sort? Were you able to observe holidays, kashrut, if it was something you needed.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was no kashrut there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about synagogues or social life or any-- any kind of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the day I left Vienna, with the exception of the time that I tried to get into the temple on Yom Kippur, I never entered another temple 'til I came to the United States. I didn't have an opportunity. I didn't have my mind set on that. As a matter of fact, the two experiences I had in Brussel, one I have no food for goyims, and the other experience I had, you gotta a ticket, turned me off.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. When you got here, to this country--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and your grandfather was Orthodox.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had this synagogue, where did you live? Did you live with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived with him for a short while.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do with your life here when you got here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when I arrived at-- in Hoboken, the ship, Dutch boat February 22nd, my grandfather greeted us. He came down. They had to get you out of the-- out of Hoboken, out of the port. And he came down with a cousin, the one that helped us to come here. She's actually only a half-cousin, because it's a half-brother of my mother's. But she came down with him. And he greeted us. The greeting was in Yiddish. [YIDDISH] When I said, no, which we didn't, between the brother-- my brother and I, we had a little over $10. He said [YIDDISH]. And he didn't believe us. And we walked out Hoboken, up stairs. And he was going to get a taxi to take us to where he lived in New York, in Manhattan. It was called Harlem. He stopped at the first taxi and he was bargaining with the guy. And it was a very warm day. The sun was out and we were wearing winter clothing and schlepping two suitcases. And we walked-- each taxi parked there. He was bargaining with them. And he-- they all gave him the same price, from $1.50 to $1.25. Finally, at the end of the block-- he was not a young man-- he took the taxi. We arrived in Harlem. He said, pay the man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I knew he wouldn't part with $10. I know already what I'm up against. He still hadn't asked my mother is, or how my father is. And-- but he was religious. Then we got up to the apartment and the doorbell was ringing and there's somebody delivering his shirts and he asked me to pay the guy with the shirts. And I said, I have no money. And that was my first experience. I arrived on a Friday. On Saturday, you don't go looking for a job. On Sunday, you don't look for a job. I found out there's such a thing as the Garment District down on University Place, 14th Street. On Monday morning, I walked down to 14th Street. I didn't want to spend any money on the subway, because God knows how long this $10 and change has to last. And I got a job in a clothing factory. I started working.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: 19. No. Wait a minute. 1940, I was 21 already. OK. My brother didn't get a job right away. And Grandpa used to have a pot and a gas flame cooking with the bare blocks in it. And I know there's a Jewish method with little pieces of meat and it would boil all day long. And I came home in the evening. And I said to him my brother was hungry, was a growing boy. And I said-- and he said he only got-- fed him once during the day. And I asked how come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said to me [YIDDISH]. That was the answer. So that was my other experience with religion. Did you-- were you accepted by the rest of the family in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was accepted by one aunt who came originally from Czechoslovakia, the mother of the cousin that was involved in getting us here. The rest of the family, the other uncles, felt-- I found out later on they felt that they were involved in-- that we might take the money that Grandfather has. He didn't. As [INAUDIBLE] in the end, the money was in the safe of his president of his shul. And when Grandpa died, he had got the money. And I know it was about $8,000 he had in there. And there' was another involvement. One of the uncles, the youngest one, Jack, who-- my brother finally got a job, delivery boy for a butcher shop on 172nd Street, who happened to be a German refugee who had a butcher shop there. My brother would deliver, get some money in tips. And he got paid $12 a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Uncle Jack came up and said, did you give the money to Grandpa? And he said, no, I didn't. He-- and I wasn't home when this happened. He said he's going to beat him up, gonna take him to the street and beat him up, and then he's going to send him back to Germany. So I came home and my brother was crying and this and that. And I had given Grandpa money. I've given some-- when I got my first pay, which was $8.56-- next pay was $15 already. I had given him money. He kissed me. And when he kissed me, I said, Grandpa, how come you kiss me now? You didn't kiss me when I came here. You didn't ask about my mother or anything else. I-- and now you kiss me because I give you money? I said, I thought you were religious. So he said to me, [YIDDISH]. You know what I'm talking about. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. You-- when we were talking earlier, you said that you went into the American Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that happen?

speaker: interviewer
text: So for three years, you worked--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked

speaker: interviewer
text: --in this country.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked and I became the fastest operator of basting machines in the clothing workers union, Local 25, the fastest one. I used to baste in a day 100 and-- over 100 Army coats, the heavy overcoats. OK? It was a nickel a coat. And when I walked out, and I would go in across the street in the barber shop and have myself-- get a shave and they put the chair back and they put the hot towel on my face, I would pass out. But I was the number one man in the union, fastest one. I joined the union, by the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And you stayed in the field and I-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not stay with my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but you stayed working. And what was your social life? Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had made some friends. First of all, for a very short time I was dating a refugee girl, very short time. Then my cousin, half-cousin, became very friendly with my brother and fell in love with him. And they had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a dance at the high school, Monroe High in the Bronx. And my cousin had skipped a year. One of the smart kids, skipped a year. My brother had the job at the butcher shop, so I was asked to take Milie to the dance. I did. And at the dance, a very pretty redhead danced over, the jitterbug, jitterbugging. And Milie introduced me, said meet my girlfriend Florence. And Florence said hi and danced off.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Milie says to me, you like my girlfriend Florence? I says, oh, she's cute, but let her grow up. After all, I wasn't 16 years old. I was 21 already. Let her grow up. Maybe two years from now I'll take her out. OK? So meanwhile I have made other friends and then some American girls I was dating. Some girls that I met at the factory I was dating. The World's Fair was going on. And through Milie I got a high school book, ticket book, which was-- so I was a real big shot. I could take girls to the World's Fair for $1.25.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would go through the gate and they said to me, you're still-- first of all, I looked much older than 21. I had black hair. And I had the beard, shave in the morning, by noontime it was black again.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, is it my fault that I'm stupid? And I got in. [LAUGHS] In the course of this period, did you have contact with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Hitler marched into Belgium. My parents fled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out later on into France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. Oh. So you had no-- you didn't know what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then I got contact a little later on. I got contact that they're being in France and my father had been interned at Gurs. My mother was in Marseille. And now they needed new papers, because the papers-- they had gotten affidavits already. But their affidavits were lost in Belgium. The American embassy, we need new papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started working on new papers. Now I had to get new people. The-- this guy-- the egg and butter man didn't want to. Had to go and get people. Then they got-- finally got affidavits.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now America is it at war, Pearl Harbor, 1941. And now my parents became enemy aliens. Now they're Germans as far as the American government is concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote a letter. A very unique thing happened. You know, it's a small world. Every Jew is related to another Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: The chief rabbi in Venice, Vienna was-- his name was Frankfurter. Frankfurter married my parents. My parents had married during the First World War. Frankfurter also was the uncle of Frankfurter, Justice Frankfurter in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Felix.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the Supreme Court. Felix Frankfurter. So I wrote to Felix Frankfurter and I got a nice letter back a few weeks later. I am sorry I can't help you as a justice of Supreme Court, I cannot become involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wrote to Mrs. Roosevelt. And it worked. Unbelievable. Within a week I got an answer that I'm going to be questioned before a commission at the State Department. I went, made an appointment about a couple of weeks later. I went to the State Department. I was questioned. All they were interested in, one thing, is my father Communist? Yeah. Five guys sitting there at the table, long table, they're questioning and questioning. Finally, it passed. He can come here. OK? Now we have another thing that's happening. We have quota systems, right? Not the American quotas. American embassy has a quota. And the quota-- my quota was because I was born in Vienna, I have a German quota, wide open.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a Polish quota. They were born with Poland. Polish Jews. They're not Austrian Jews. They're Polish Jews. That quota is only-- I forgot already what it was, a very small quota. And there-- for everyone that wanted to come here, there were 10 that couldn't make it. But finally, Pearl Harbor is here, everything else. One ship is coming to the United States a month, a little Spanish ship from Marseille, lands in Bermuda and then comes here. The tickets are $1,100. I had $800 saved.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the [INAUDIBLE], no way. They wouldn't advance any money. I'm short $300. They're the ones that get you the tickets. You have to buy it through the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you going to do? Went to the bank. I had my uncle, my mother's brother, co-sign. I got the $300, brought it to the [INAUDIBLE]. Now we had $1,100 dollars and I think it's time. Now we're 1942 already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Had a ticket to go to be on the ship. When the ship gets to Bermuda, they have an advance passenger list, you're not on the ship. I came up to the State Department in Washington, DC. Within a few days, I got an answer. The embassy in Marseilles-- the consul, not the embassy. You have been deported to days before or three days before the ship left, you were deported to German proper. After the war, we found out German proper was Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to change our tape again now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.
